---
title: "W07-Reflection Essay-Introduction to Positro"
author: "Angelina"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: darkly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-expand: 3
    toc-title: "Table of Contents"
    number-sections: false
    self-contained: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: dracula
    smooth-scroll: true
    css: styles.css
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# 1. Prepare Your Report {#sec-prepare}

> When you prepare the Quarto report, implement as many features and effects of the HTML format as you can, including, but not limited to, the following:

-   Text formatting
-   Headings
-   Lists
-   Blockquote
-   Panel Tabset or Tables
-   Cross-referencing
-   Footnote
-   Callout blocks
-   Table of Contents
-   toc-depth / toc-expand
-   Section Numbering
-   Self-contained
-   HTML theme options
-   Hiding code
-   Code overflow
-   Code tools

# 2. Essay Responses {#sec-essay}

## 2.1. Download and Install Positron. As you watch the videos in Step 1 and Step 3, follow the activities in the video and be familiar with Positron. {#sec-install}

I successfully downloaded and installed Positron following the instructions from the link of the beginning of the module.

## 2.2. Based on what you learned from Step 1 and Step 3, what do you like about Positron compared with RStudio?

::: panel-tabset
### Step 1: Observations

What I liked from Step 1:

-   **Git integration** is significantly easier in Positron than RStudio. The left sidebar has a dedicated Git panel that makes committing, pushing, and branching much more intuitive.
-   **Built-in AI assistant**: RStudio does not have this. Being able to ask questions about my code *inside* the IDE without switching to a browser is a time-saver.
-   The AI understands my **files**: it can see my variables and data, making suggestions much more relevant than a generic chatbot.

### Step 3: Observations

What I liked from Step 3:

-   **Customizable layouts**: RStudio's layout is fairly rigid. Positron lets you drag and rearrange panels however you want, which makes the workspace feel more personal and efficient.
-   **Modern GUI**: Positron looks and feels more like a contemporary developer tool compared to RStudio's older aesthetic.
-   **R + Python support**: If I ever want to explore Python, I can do it in the same IDE without switching tools.
:::

### Side-by-Side Feature Comparison {#sec-table}

| Feature              | RStudio | Positron   |
|----------------------|---------|------------|
| AI Assistant         | ☒       | ☑ Built-in |
| Python Support       | Limited | ☑ Full     |
| Git Panel            | Basic   | ☑ Advanced |
| Layout Customization | ☒ Fixed | ☑ Flexible |
| Built on VS Code     | ☒       | ☑          |
| Open Source          | ☑       | ☑          |
| Databot Extension    | ☒       | ☑          |

: Comparison of RStudio and Positron features {#tbl-comparison}

See @tbl-comparison for a full feature breakdown between the two IDEs.

## 2.3. In Step 4, the video demonstrates how you can use AI. {#sec-ai}

### 2.3.1. Overview of AI Tools {#sec-ai-overview}

Positron offers several ways to use AI, ranging from free to paid options depending on the provider you connect.[^1]

[^1]: Costs vary based on API usage. Free-tier API keys from some providers may work for light use.

:::: panel-tabset
### Positron Assistant

The Positron Assistant is a built-in AI client integrated directly into the IDE. It has access to my full workplace like active scripts, variables in memory, and console errors.

**Key features:**

1.  **Sidebar Chat**: A dedicated chat interface for asking questions, debugging code, or generating new snippets from scratch.
2.  **Inline Edits**: Press `Cmd+I` to open a prompt *directly inside the code editor* to modify existing code (e.g., *"Make this plot red"* or *"Label the outliers"*).
3.  **Model Agnostic**: Supports multiple AI providers:
    -   Anthropic (Claude) ☑
    -   AWS Bedrock ☑
    -   Google Gemini ☑
    -   GitHub Copilot ☑

::: callout-important
## Cost

Positron IDE is free and open-source. But you need your own API key to power the AI (in the settings) and most providers charge based on usage.
:::

### Databot Extension

Databot is a extension designed specifically for fast exploratory data analysis. It operates more autonomously than the built-in assistant.

**Key capabilities:**

-   **Autonomous EDA**: You can give complex instructions like *"Combine these five CSVs and find the correlation between GDP and happiness"* and Databot writes and runs the code.
-   **The SWEAR Loop**: Databot follows a cycle of writing → executing → analyzing → regrouping until the task is complete.
-   **Auditability**: Every action is code-based. You can review, approve, or reject code before it runs.
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.3.2. Which AI tools have you installed or set up? Which AI tools did you find beneficial for you? {#sec-ai-setup}

I have set up the Positron Assistant connected to my Anthropic (Claude) API key.

::: callout-tip
## Why I find it beneficial

Instead of copying and pasting error messages into a separate browser, I can get help directly inside my script. The assistant understands everything, like my variable names, my data structure, my actual errors.
:::

**Specific ways that I can use it:**

-   Fixing errors instantly by showing it my console output
-   Writing new plot code without leaving the IDE
-   Asking "what does this function do?" without Googling or going to Quarto guide.

### 2.4. I strongly recommend using GitHub Copilot, which is free if you apply for an education account {#sec-copilot}

#### 2.4.1. Apply for it and take a screenshot showing you were accepted into the education program {#sec-copilot-apply}

I applied for the GitHub Education.

![GitHub Education Acceptance Screenshot](github.png){#fig-github fig-alt="Screenshot showing GitHub Education program acceptance" fig-align="left"}

See @fig-github for the acceptance confirmation screenshot.

#### 2.4.2. Play with it and do you find it helpful or distracting? Please elaborate {#sec-copilot-experience}

::: panel-tabset
### Helpful Aspects

GitHub Copilot feels like having a tutor sitting next to me. Specific things I found helpful:

-   It explains my mistakes *as I make them*, not just after I ask.
-   Auto-completing repetitive code patterns saves a lot of typing.
-   The inline suggestions mean I spend less time context-switching between coding and searching.

Distracting Aspects

It can be overwhelming at first:

-   There are so many apps and offers in the GitHub Education pack that I don't understand yet
-   Sometimes I spend more time exploring the free tools than actually coding.
-   The suggestions occasionally interrupt my thought process when I already know what I want to write.
:::

::: callout-warning
## Balance is Key

Like any tool, Copilot works best when you already have some coding knowledge. But I think if I rely on it too heavily early on can slow down me learning the fundamentals.
:::

# 3. Publish this report to GitHub Pages and provide a URL to the GitHub Pages for the report. Note that although both GitHub Pages and GitHub repo are online, they are different. GitHub Pages is a website publishing service that hosts the rendered HTML from a QMD file. {#sec-publish}

This report has been published to GitHub Pages. The rendered HTML is hosted at:

[github.com/angelinagjao/positron-report](github.com/angelinagjao/positron-report)
